Research

Towards the next generation of gravitational-wave detectors through large-scale trade studies


Current generation gravitational-wave detectors, namely LIGO, Virgo, and KAGRA, have successfully ushered in the era of gravitational-wave astronomy. However, having been conceptualized in the 1970s and biult in the 1990s, their limitations in sensitivity and bandwidth will limit how many and which sources can be observed and at what fidelity. Therefore, the gravitational-wave community commenced the exploration, design, and development of next-generation detectors which are envisioned to be an order of magnitude more sensitive. I have been involved in several investigations of the science case for these detectors, particularly through large-scale trade studies, that explored the impact of different detector designs, configurations, and also the placement on Earth. We were able to scrutinize various design choices for the Einstein Telescope and Cosmic Explorer, the two leading, next-generation detector proposals. These detectors would facilitate a dramatic increase in the number of observable compact binary coalescences, from currently ~100 to ~100,000 per year, yield much louder detections with signal-to-noise ratios jumping an order of magnitude in acccordance with the sensitivity improvements, and provide such clean data with vastly improved measurement quality: enabling precision cosmology and tests of general relativity and simply a deeper understanding of the astrophysical processes and origins governing these sources.

arXiv:2109.09882 arXiv:2202.11048 arXiv:2212.13183 arXiv:2301.08763 arXiv:2303.15923 arXiv:2306.13745 arXiv:2307.10421 arXiv:2503.12263 arXiv:2510.11861

Resolution of the Hubble-Lemaître tension with a single, golden Dark siren


Besides tests of GR, GW observations of compact binary mergers also play a unique role for cosmology: They facilitate the measurement of the Hubble constant through a luminosity distance estimate in combination with an independent, EM redshift measurement from the GW source's host galaxy. In the light of the tension between the EM measurements of the Hubble constant from the early and late universe, the GW-assisted method has gained even more importance. While other studies investigated methods to resolve the Hubble-Lemaître tension with binary neutron stars, we demonstrated that there is an observable population of golden dark sirens that could facilitate this resolution in the next five years without direct EM counterparts or statistical methods. Instead, improved waveform models using higher spherical harmonic modes and planned upgrades to GW detectors will compound together to localize these golden events with high-precision, in distance and on the sky, to uniquely determine their host galaxies in the local universe and consequently measure the Hubble constant to the required precision.

Astrophys. J. Lett., 905, L28 (2020) — arXiv:2007.02883 Publicity | AAS Nova Publicity | Live Science Publicity | Vice

Connection of the post-Newtonian and strong-field regimes


The connection of the inspiraling progenitor system and the ringing remnant black hole is one of the open problems of BBH merger modeling. Following previous studies we examined the amplitudes of the GWs emitted during BBH mergers from the late inspiral to the early ringdown and modeled the imprint of the progenitor system throughout the strong-field regime. In doing so, we compared the analytical predictions of post-Newtonian (PN) theory to the data provided by numerical relativity simulations of the SXS BBH catalog. We found a remarkable agreement of the dominant spherical harmonic mode amplitudes with the functional dependence on progenitor parameters as described in PN theory. This PN signature was not expected and indicates a potential for waveform model development in the strong-field regime. Further such models can be useful to develop no-hair tests of the merger remnant's black hole nature.

Class. Quant. Grav., 37, 065006 (2020) — arXiv:1901.08516
